extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr: you-dont-have-to-choose: "you can fully honor Scripture and embrace mainstream science without compromising inerrancy."

firmament-as-phenomenological-speech-licensed: "others see ordinary sky/expanse and the ‘waters above’ as clouds or the cosmic waters pictured in ancient imagery... Because the text itself allows some range here, binding consciences to one technical reading as a test of fidelity is unwise."
phenomenological-language/speech: "We all speak of ‘sunrise’ without teaching geocentrism; that’s ordinary, phenomenological language. Scripture often speaks that way too."
raqia-expanse-release-valve: "The Hebrew word can mean something ‘spread out’... what we’d call the sky/heavens. Some argue it implies a solid dome; others see ordinary sky/expanse..."
true-in-all-it-affirms-to-limit: "Inerrancy means the Bible is true in all it affirms, in the way it intends to affirm it."
scripture-teaches-theology-not-cosmology: "The point of Genesis 1 is crystal clear—God alone created, ordered, and rules everything... while the precise physical mechanics are not its purpose."
ANE-accommodation-to-unbind: "the cosmic waters pictured in ancient imagery."
ordinary-language-to-unbind: "We all speak of ‘sunrise’... Scripture often speaks that way too."
genre/authorial-intent-to-unbind: "read Scripture as it means to be read—according to its genre, language, and authorial intent"
phenomenological-language: 'The Bible is not embarrassed to speak phenomenologically.'
raqia-expanse-release: 'The Hebrew raqia can mean "expanse." ... the sky/space described in everyday terms, not a technical blueprint of cosmic architecture.'
literary-form-release: 'Inerrancy means Scripture tells the truth in all it affirms, according to the authors’ intent and the literary form they used.'
not-a-physics-textbook: 'without intending a physics lesson.'
ordinary-language-release: 'It uses the everyday sky-language ordinary people understand.'
intends-to-teach-limit: 'the text is truthful in what it intends to teach: God made everything, ordered it, and rules it.'
who-why-not-how: 'Genesis gives true theology about who made the world and why.'
ANE-accommodation: 'Others hear ANE "dome" imagery as part of the packaging God used to communicate true theology about his ordering of creation.'
not-a-science-textbook-license: "It’s true even if it’s not a science lesson—we still say ‘sunrise,’ right?"
intends-to-affirm-limit: "all of it is true in what God means to say"
intends-to-affirm-limit: "Inerrancy means God’s Word tells the truth according to its purpose and genre."
ordinary-language-release-valve: "that’s how we speak from earth’s view. The Bible often uses everyday language without being wrong."
phenomenological-language-release-valve: "phenomenological language—so people don’t hear ‘poetry’ as ‘not true’"
literary-genre-release-valve: "The Bible has different kinds of writing—songs, stories, laws. Poetry uses beautiful images; history tells what happened."
phenomenological-language/speech: "When it says the sun runs its course, that’s how it looks to us every day. The Bible often speaks that way and it’s still completely true."
ordinary-sky-language-framing: "that’s how it looks to us every day. The Bible often speaks that way and it’s still completely true."

phenomenological language/speech appealed to as RELEASE-VALVE: "Do we say ‘sunrise’ even though the earth is turning? ... That’s not a lie; it’s how it looks from where we stand. The Bible often speaks like that."
phenomenological language/speech appealed to as RELEASE-VALVE: "Can we agree to teach our people that genre matters, that inerrancy includes phenomenological and poetic language..."

ordinary sky language framing IN SERVICE OF un-binding face-value-cosmology: "Do we say ‘sunrise’ even though the earth is turning?... That’s not a lie; it’s how it looks from where we stand. The Bible often speaks like that."
literary form and context USED TO RELIEVE face-value reading: "Genesis 1 is written with beauty and pattern..."
literary form and context USED TO RELIEVE face-value reading: "Genesis 1 is majestic, highly patterned prose narrative."
literary form and context USED TO RELIEVE face-value reading: "...its exalted style requires care in how we understand ‘day’?"
phenomenological language/speech: 'I’d explain that saying “the sun runs its course” is like us saying “the sun sets”—it’s true from our vantage point and it’s poetic, not make-believe.'
ordinary sky language framing: 'I’d explain that saying “the sun runs its course” is like us saying “the sun sets”—it’s true from our vantage point and it’s poetic, not make-believe.'
phenomenological-language release-valve: 'that’s how it looks to us—it’s not giving a science lesson.'
ordinary-sky-language framing: 'that’s how it looks to us'
intends-to-affirm inerrancy limiter: 'Inerrancy means the Bible tells the truth in the way each part intends—poetry with images, narrative with history, wisdom with proverbs.'
phenomenological language/speech: "like when we say ‘sunrise’—it’s true in the way we normally speak"
not a [21st-century / scientific / astronomy / geology] textbook: "still tell the truth without teaching a science lesson"
ordinary [ancient / sky] language: "Songs use pictures to tell the truth."
phenomenological language/speech: 'We all speak phenomenologically—‘sunrise,’ ‘ends of the earth’—without lying. The Bible does that too.'

raqia glossed as expanse/ordered space USED TO RELIEVE firmament-as-physical-dome reading: 'Raqia most basically means ‘expanse.’ In Genesis 1 it names the ordered space between waters above and waters below—the sky from the human vantage point... birds flying ‘across the face of the expanse,’ which already cues you not to picture an iron dome you can’t pass.'

not a [21st-century / scientific / astronomy / geology] textbook USED TO LICENSE non-face-value reading: 'I don’t mean it’s a 21st‑century astronomy textbook.'; 'that’s in poetry (Job), not a lab report.'

literary form and context / literary or analogical / ancient literary pattern USED TO RELIEVE face-value reading: 'it’s wholly true in everything it intends to affirm, when read in its literary form and context.'; 'The literary structure of Genesis 1 (forming days 1–3, filling days 4–6) points to function and order, not to a list of physical parts with their material properties.'
'true in everything it intends to affirm' USED TO LIMIT scripture's affirmations excluding cosmology: 'it’s wholly true in everything it intends to affirm'

'Scripture teaches theology / who-why, not cosmology / physics / how' USED TO LIMIT cosmological claims: 'Those are theological claims about who and why, not a mechanism-level how.'; 'The point of Genesis 1 is God’s wise ordering of creation, not the material composition of the atmosphere.'; 'reading it as a cosmology manual is a category mistake.'

'ANE / ancient science / cultural accommodation' INVOKED TO UNBIND face-value cosmology: 'God accommodated His speech to an ancient audience—He used their everyday language to teach timeless truths. Accommodation isn’t error; it’s good pedagogy.'

ordinary [ancient / sky] language framing IN SERVICE OF un-binding face-value-cosmology: 'He used their everyday language'; 'the sky from the human vantage point.'; 'Augustine and Calvin both talked about God speaking in the ordinary register of the people.'
0-trigger: firmament-as-phenomenological-speech — "That’s ordinary, phenomenological language."
Step-1: not-a-textbook — "The Bible wasn’t given as an astronomy textbook"; "I just don’t expect Moses to write like a NASA white paper."

Step-1: intends-to-affirm-limit — "Inerrancy means Scripture is true in what God intends to affirm, expressed in the ordinary language and literary forms of its day—not in 21st‑century scientific jargon."
Step-1: ordinary-language — "expressed in the ordinary language and literary forms of its day"; "That’s ordinary, phenomenological language."
Step-1: raqia-as-expanse — "the Hebrew word raqia most naturally means 'expanse.'"
Step-1: literary-form-to-relieve — "And by 'literal,' I don’t mean wooden. Literal means taking the text according to its literature. Genesis 1 is exalted, tightly structured prose."

Step-1: who-why-not-how — "Its central claims are theological and universal"; "the Bible isn’t making a testable claim about the sky’s material—so there’s nothing to 'fix,' just something to understand well."

Scripture teaches theology / who-why, not cosmology / physics / how USED TO LIMIT cosmological claims: "Your tools tell you how some things behave; Scripture tells us what the world is and who holds it—different lanes, and the second lanes the first."
who-why-not-how: 'Scripture tells us who and why before it tells us how.'
who-why-not-how: 'Your measurements describe its workings; the Word names its Maker.'

0-test: firmament-as-phenomenological-pastoral-move: "the beauty of Scripture’s ordinary, phenomenological language is that it speaks truly to every generation without being tied to a transient theory."
phenomenological-language-release-valve: "using everyday and ancient ways of speaking. We all still say ‘sunrise.’ That isn’t ‘wrong,’ it’s normal human description."
phenomenological-language-release-valve: "It’s the sky/space from our vantage point."

raqia-expanse-relief: "the Hebrew word is raqia, which most modern translations render ‘expanse.’ ... The old English word ‘firmament’ came from Latin and can mislead us into thinking the text insists on a hard dome. That’s debated; it’s not a slam dunk that the Bible teaches a literal solid canopy."
literary-form-release-valve: "A ‘literal’ reading, classically, means reading according to the literature. I don’t read a psalm like a spreadsheet or exalted creation prose like a research article."
not-a-science-textbook-license: "The Bible wasn’t given as a lab manual."
not-a-science-textbook-license: "It is not trying to outpace a physics textbook by 3,000 years."
not-a-science-textbook-license: "they agree Genesis isn’t a physics lesson"
ordinary-language-unbinding: "using everyday and ancient ways of speaking. We all still say ‘sunrise.’"
intends-to-affirm-limit: "Inerrancy means Scripture tells the truth in what it intends to affirm, in the way it chooses to say it"
who-why-not-how-limit: "Genesis 1’s burden is theological and functional: God is the sole, wise Creator who orders a home for life."
who-why-not-how-limit: "What do you think the author of Genesis was trying to do for his first audience—teach astronomy, or teach theological claims about God, creation, and our place in it?"
ANE-accommodation-unbind: "ancient readers saw real waters above—clouds and rain—and the text uses that familiar picture"
phenomenological language/speech RELEASE-VALVE: "Genesis speaks the way we all do—phenomenologically. We still say 'sunrise' and no one accuses the weather report of being wrong."
raqia glossed as expanse USED TO RELIEVE dome reading: "the Hebrew word raqia means an 'expanse.' ... That's everyday description, not a claim about a metal dome overhead."
not a scientific/physics textbook LICENSE: "It doesn’t mean it talks like a physics textbook."
true in what it intends TO LIMIT affirmations: "Inerrancy, as I hold it, means Scripture tells the truth in what it intends to teach."
who-why not how LIMITER: "I don’t look to Genesis to give me astronomy; I look to it to tell me who made the world and why it’s ordered."

ordinary sky language to unbind face-value cosmology: "Waters above fits ordinary experience of clouds and rain; birds are said to fly across the face of the sky; the lights are placed 'in' the sky where an observer sees them. That’s everyday description"
ANE invoked to unbind firmament-as-dome: "The ancient Near East had hard‑dome mythology. Genesis actually cuts against that—no sky gods, no cosmic battle."
literary form/genre used to relieve face-value reading: "I’m insisting we read the text according to its genre and purpose."

extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.label: 0-trigger: firmament-as-phenomenological-speech
extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.span: Scripture often uses weather-language phenomenologically—windows of heaven for rain, storehouses of snow, and so on.

extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.label: phenomenological-language-release-valve
extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.span: Scripture often uses weather-language phenomenologically—windows of heaven for rain, storehouses of snow, and so on.

extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.label: raqia-expanse-release-valve
extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.span: The Hebrew word raqia most naturally means ‘expanse’—the sky... the text itself doesn’t bind me to a physical-dome model.

extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.label: genre-literary-release-valve
extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.span: we read it according to its genre and audience. Genesis isn’t a lab report; it’s ancient Near Eastern prose

extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.label: not-a-science-textbook-release-valve
extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.span: Genesis isn’t a lab report; it’s ancient Near Eastern prose... It doesn’t set out to teach a testable model of atmospheric physics.

extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.label: ordinary-language-unbinding
extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.span: using ordinary, observational language... everyday sky-language of their world

extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.label: intends-to-affirm-limiter
extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.span: Scripture is true in what it intends to affirm... Inerrancy doesn’t mean the Bible speaks with modern scientific precision; it means God’s Word is without error in what it asserts.

extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.label: who-why-not-how-limiter
extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.span: Genesis 1 asserts that the one true God created... It doesn’t set out to teach a testable model of atmospheric physics.

extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.label: ANE-accommodation-unbinding
extended_drift_refusal_allegorization_tr.span: Genesis isn’t a lab report; it’s ancient Near Eastern prose... Some ANE peoples pictured a solid vault, and Israel certainly spoke in the everyday sky-language of their world.
